title: Board Oversight of Technology Risk
verbatim: The board of directors shall maintain effective oversight of technology risk management across the financial institution. The board shall ensure that technology risk is integrated into the enterprise risk management framework and that adequate resources are allocated for technology risk management activities.
translation: The board must actively oversee how the institution manages technology risks. This means technology risk should be part of the overall enterprise risk framework, and the board must ensure enough budget, people, and tools are dedicated to managing it.
evidence: Board meeting minutes discussing technology risk
Enterprise risk management framework incorporating technology risk
Board-approved technology risk budget and resource allocation
Board terms of reference covering technology risk oversight

title: Board Technology Risk Committee
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a board-level committee responsible for overseeing technology risk. The committee shall comprise members with sufficient knowledge and experience in technology and cybersecurity matters, and shall convene at least quarterly to review the institution's technology risk posture.
translation: You need a dedicated board committee focused on technology risk. Its members must understand technology and cyber topics, and it must meet at least every quarter to review the current state of technology risk.
evidence: Board committee charter or terms of reference
Committee member profiles demonstrating relevant expertise
Quarterly meeting schedules and attendance records

title: Board Competency in Technology
verbatim: Financial institutions should ensure that members of the board of directors possess or have access to adequate knowledge and expertise in technology and cybersecurity. The board should undertake continuous education and training to remain informed of evolving technology risks and emerging threats.
translation: Board members should either have personal expertise in technology and cybersecurity, or have access to advisors who do. Ongoing training and education programs should keep the board current on new threats and technology trends.

title: Board Risk Appetite Statement
verbatim: The board shall approve a technology risk appetite statement that defines the level of technology risk the financial institution is willing to accept. The risk appetite shall be expressed using quantitative and qualitative measures and shall be reviewed at least annually to reflect changes in the operating environment.
translation: The board must formally approve a statement that sets out how much technology risk the institution is prepared to tolerate. This should include both measurable metrics and qualitative descriptions, and must be reviewed yearly or when circumstances change significantly.
evidence: Evidence of risk appetite communication to management

artifacts.description: Board-approved statement defining acceptable levels of technology risk

artifacts.description: Periodic report on actual risk levels versus approved appetite thresholds

title: Board Reporting on Technology Risk
verbatim: Senior management shall provide the board with comprehensive and timely reports on technology risk, including key risk indicators, incident summaries, and the status of risk mitigation initiatives. Reporting shall occur at least quarterly and shall include trend analysis and forward-looking assessments.
translation: Management must give the board regular, detailed reports on technology risk at least every quarter. Reports must cover KRIs, incident trends, remediation progress, and forward-looking risk assessments so the board can make informed decisions.
evidence: Quarterly technology risk reports to the board
Incident summary reports presented to board

title: Senior Management Accountability
verbatim: Senior management shall be accountable for implementing the technology risk management framework approved by the board. Senior management shall designate a Chief Information Security Officer or equivalent role with direct reporting access to the board or board committee on cybersecurity matters.
translation: Senior management is directly responsible for putting the board-approved technology risk framework into practice. They must appoint a CISO (or equivalent) who can report directly to the board or its technology risk committee on cybersecurity issues.
evidence: Senior management accountability matrix for technology risk
CISO appointment letter and job description
Board or committee meeting records showing CISO reporting

title: Technology Risk Management Function
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish an independent technology risk management function with adequate authority, resources, and access to information. The function shall be independent from technology operations and shall be responsible for identifying, assessing, monitoring, and reporting technology risks.
translation: You must have a dedicated technology risk management team that operates independently from the IT operations team. This team needs proper authority, staffing, and access to systems and data to do its job of identifying, assessing, monitoring, and reporting technology risks.
evidence: Organisation chart showing independence from IT operations

title: Technology Risk Framework
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish and maintain a comprehensive technology risk management framework that is commensurate with the nature, size, and complexity of the institution's operations. The framework shall define risk governance structures, risk appetite, risk assessment methodologies, and risk reporting mechanisms.
translation: You must create and maintain a technology risk management framework that fits your institution's size and complexity. It should cover governance structures, risk appetite, how you assess risks, and how you report on them.

title: Risk Identification & Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct regular and systematic identification and assessment of technology risks. Risk assessments shall consider threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, and potential impact across all critical technology assets and services. Assessments shall be performed at least annually and upon significant changes.
translation: You must regularly and systematically identify and assess technology risks by looking at threats, vulnerabilities, how likely they are, and what impact they could have. This applies to all critical technology assets and must happen at least yearly or whenever there are major changes.
evidence: Risk assessment schedule and completion records

title: Risk Treatment & Mitigation
verbatim: Financial institutions shall develop and implement risk treatment plans for identified technology risks. Risk treatment options shall include avoidance, mitigation, transfer, or acceptance, with formal approval for risk acceptance by the appropriate authority level. Treatment plans shall include timelines and accountable owners.
translation: For every identified technology risk, you need a treatment plan: avoid it, mitigate it, transfer it, or accept it. Risk acceptance requires formal sign-off by someone with appropriate authority. Each plan must have deadlines and a named person responsible.
evidence: Risk treatment plans with assigned owners
Risk acceptance approvals at appropriate authority level

title: Risk Monitoring & Reporting
verbatim: Financial institutions should establish mechanisms for continuous monitoring and periodic reporting of technology risks. Key risk indicators should be defined, measured, and reported to senior management and the board. Breaches of risk appetite thresholds should trigger escalation procedures.
translation: You should set up ongoing monitoring and regular reporting of technology risks. Define KRIs that are tracked and reported to management and the board. When risk thresholds are breached, there should be automatic escalation processes.
evidence: Key risk indicator definitions and thresholds

title: Technology Risk Register
verbatim: Financial institutions shall maintain a comprehensive and up-to-date technology risk register. The register shall document all identified technology risks, their assessed likelihood and impact, risk owners, treatment plans, and current status. The register shall be reviewed at least quarterly.
translation: You must keep a complete, current register of all technology risks. Each entry needs the risk description, likelihood, impact, owner, treatment plan, and status. This register must be reviewed at least every quarter to ensure it reflects reality.

Quarterly review records of the risk register

title: IT Project Governance
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a governance framework for all technology projects that includes defined roles and responsibilities, approval gates, and oversight mechanisms. Major technology projects shall be subject to independent risk assessment and board or senior management approval prior to commencement.
translation: You need a formal governance framework for IT projects with clear roles, stage-gate approvals, and oversight. Major projects must undergo an independent risk assessment and get board or senior management approval before they start.

title: Project Risk Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct risk assessments for all technology projects to identify potential risks to security, availability, and data integrity. The assessment shall consider interdependencies with existing systems and the potential impact on customers and business operations.
translation: Every IT project must have a risk assessment covering security, availability, and data integrity. You also need to consider how the project interacts with existing systems and what impact it could have on customers and day-to-day operations.

title: Post-Implementation Review
verbatim: Financial institutions should conduct post-implementation reviews for all significant technology projects to assess whether project objectives were achieved, identify lessons learned, and evaluate the effectiveness of risk mitigation measures. Reviews should be completed within three months of project go-live.
translation: After major IT projects go live, you should conduct a formal review within three months to check if objectives were met, capture lessons learned, and evaluate whether risk controls are working as intended.

title: SDLC Framework
verbatim: Financial institutions shall adopt a formal software development life cycle (SDLC) framework that integrates security at every phase. The framework shall include requirements gathering, design, development, testing, deployment, and maintenance phases with defined security checkpoints and quality gates.
translation: You must use a formal SDLC framework where security is built into every phase from requirements through to maintenance. Each phase must have defined security checkpoints and quality gates that must be passed before proceeding.

Evidence of framework adherence in recent projects

title: Requirements Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall ensure that security and compliance requirements are explicitly defined and documented during the requirements phase of system development. Requirements shall be traceable throughout the development lifecycle and validated prior to deployment.
translation: Security and compliance requirements must be clearly documented at the start of every development project. These requirements must be traceable through design, build, and test so you can prove each one was implemented and validated before go-live.

title: Secure Development Standards
verbatim: Financial institutions shall adopt and enforce secure coding standards based on recognised industry practices such as OWASP. Developers shall be trained on secure coding practices and compliance with these standards shall be verified through code reviews and automated scanning tools.
translation: You must have secure coding standards based on industry best practices like OWASP. All developers must be trained on them, and you must verify compliance through both manual code reviews and automated scanning tools.

Code review records referencing coding standards
Automated scanning tool configuration and results

title: Code Review & Static Analysis
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement mandatory code review processes and static application security testing (SAST) for all application code prior to deployment to production. Critical and high-severity findings shall be remediated before release, and exceptions shall require formal risk acceptance.
translation: All code must go through both peer code reviews and automated static analysis (SAST) before it reaches production. Any critical or high-severity issues found must be fixed before release. If you cannot fix them, you need formal documented risk acceptance.
evidence: SAST tool configuration and scan results

title: Testing Standards
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish comprehensive testing standards that include functional testing, integration testing, performance testing, and security testing. Test plans shall be documented and test results shall be formally reviewed and approved prior to production deployment.
translation: You must have formal testing standards covering functional, integration, performance, and security testing. Test plans must be documented upfront, and test results must be reviewed and approved before anything goes to production.
evidence: Test plan templates and completed test plans
Test execution results and defect reports

title: UAT & Acceptance
verbatim: Financial institutions shall ensure that user acceptance testing (UAT) is conducted by business users in an environment that mirrors production. UAT results shall be formally documented and signed off by business owners prior to production deployment. UAT environments shall not contain production data unless appropriately masked.
translation: Business users must conduct UAT in an environment that closely resembles production. Results must be formally documented and signed off by business owners before go-live. If production data is used in UAT, it must be properly masked or anonymised.
evidence: UAT environment specification and comparison to production
UAT sign-off records from business owners

title: Change Management Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a formal change management policy that governs all changes to production systems, including applications, infrastructure, and configurations. All changes shall be assessed for risk, tested, approved by authorised personnel, and documented in a change log.
translation: Every change to production systems — applications, infrastructure, or configurations — must go through a formal change management process. Each change needs a risk assessment, testing, authorised approval, and must be logged in a change register.
evidence: Change advisory board terms of reference
Sample change records with risk assessment and approvals

title: Emergency Change Procedures
verbatim: Financial institutions shall define procedures for emergency changes that bypass normal change management processes. Emergency changes shall be subject to retrospective review and approval within a defined timeframe. The use of emergency change procedures shall be monitored and reported to management.
translation: You need specific procedures for handling urgent changes that cannot wait for the normal process. Emergency changes must be retrospectively reviewed and approved within a set timeframe, and management must receive reports on how often emergency changes are used.
evidence: Emergency change records with retrospective approvals

title: Release Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a release management process that ensures controlled and orderly deployment of changes to production environments. Release plans shall include rollback procedures, deployment verification steps, and defined success criteria.
translation: You must have a structured release management process for deploying changes to production. Every release plan needs rollback procedures in case something goes wrong, verification steps to confirm the deployment worked, and clear criteria for what constitutes a successful release.

title: Environment Separation
verbatim: Financial institutions shall maintain strict separation between development, testing, staging, and production environments. Access to production environments shall be restricted and controlled. Migration of code between environments shall follow a defined and auditable process.
translation: Development, testing, staging, and production must be clearly separated environments. Access to production must be tightly restricted, and moving code between environments must follow a formal, auditable process with proper controls.

title: Source Code Protection
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement adequate controls to protect source code from unauthorised access, modification, and loss. Source code repositories shall be access-controlled and changes shall be tracked through version control systems. Source code shall not be stored in production environments.
translation: Source code must be protected from unauthorised access and changes. Use version-controlled repositories with proper access controls, track all changes, and ensure source code is never stored directly on production servers.

title: Configuration Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish configuration management processes to maintain an accurate and complete inventory of all technology assets, their configurations, and interdependencies. Configuration baselines shall be established and deviations shall be detected, assessed, and remediated.
translation: You must manage configurations formally — keeping an accurate inventory of all technology assets and their settings. Establish approved configuration baselines and have processes to detect, assess, and fix any deviations from those baselines.

title: Capacity Planning
verbatim: Financial institutions should establish capacity planning processes to ensure that technology resources are sufficient to meet current and projected business requirements. Capacity thresholds should be defined and monitored, with timely scaling or procurement actions taken when thresholds are approached.
translation: You should plan technology capacity to ensure systems can handle current and future demand. Define capacity thresholds, monitor them continuously, and take action to scale up or procure more resources before limits are reached.

title: Patch Management Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a formal patch management policy that defines the processes for identifying, evaluating, testing, and deploying patches across all technology assets. The policy shall define patch classification criteria and maximum timeframes for deployment based on severity.
translation: You must have a written patch management policy covering how patches are identified, evaluated, tested, and deployed. The policy must classify patches by severity and set maximum timeframes for each — for example, critical patches within 14 days, high within 30 days.

title: Patch Deployment Timeline
verbatim: Financial institutions shall deploy critical security patches within fourteen days of release and high-severity patches within thirty days. Where immediate patching is not feasible, compensating controls shall be implemented and documented, with formal risk acceptance by the appropriate authority.
translation: Critical patches must be applied within 14 days and high-severity patches within 30 days. If you truly cannot patch in time, you must implement compensating controls, document them, and get formal risk acceptance from the appropriate authority level.
evidence: Compensating control documentation for delayed patches
Risk acceptance records for patch exceptions

title: End-of-Life Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall maintain an inventory of all technology assets approaching or past end-of-life and end-of-support status. A migration or replacement plan shall be established for all end-of-life systems. Where end-of-life systems remain in use, compensating controls and formal risk acceptance shall be documented.
translation: You must track all systems approaching or past end-of-life or end-of-support dates. Each one needs a migration or replacement plan. If an EOL system cannot be replaced yet, you need documented compensating controls and formal risk acceptance.
evidence: Migration or replacement plans for EOL systems

title: Cryptographic Policy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a cryptographic policy that defines the approved cryptographic algorithms, key lengths, and protocols for protecting data at rest and in transit. The policy shall be aligned with recognised international standards and reviewed at least annually to address cryptographic advancements and vulnerabilities.
translation: You need a written cryptographic policy specifying which algorithms, key lengths, and protocols are approved for protecting data at rest and in transit. Align it with international standards (like NIST) and review it yearly to stay current with evolving threats.

title: Key Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement a key management framework that governs the entire lifecycle of cryptographic keys, including generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction. Keys shall be protected using hardware security modules or equivalent mechanisms for critical systems.
translation: Manage cryptographic keys through their full lifecycle — generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction. For critical systems, keys must be stored in hardware security modules (HSMs) or similarly secure mechanisms.

title: Encryption Standards
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement encryption for all sensitive data at rest and in transit using approved cryptographic algorithms with a minimum key length of 256 bits for symmetric encryption and 2048 bits for asymmetric encryption. Deprecated or weak algorithms shall be identified and replaced.
translation: All sensitive data must be encrypted both at rest and in transit. Use AES-256 or equivalent for symmetric encryption and at least RSA-2048 for asymmetric. Identify and replace any deprecated or weak algorithms still in use.

title: Certificate Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish processes for managing digital certificates, including issuance, renewal, revocation, and monitoring of certificate expiry. Certificates shall be obtained from trusted certificate authorities and certificate transparency shall be monitored to detect unauthorised issuance.
translation: You must manage digital certificates through their lifecycle — issue, renew, revoke, and monitor them for expiry. Use trusted certificate authorities and monitor certificate transparency logs to detect any unauthorised certificates issued for your domains.

title: Data Centre Physical Security
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement physical security controls for data centres that include perimeter security, multi-layer access controls, CCTV surveillance with a minimum retention of ninety days, and visitor management procedures. Physical access shall be restricted to authorised personnel only.
translation: Data centres must have multiple layers of physical security: perimeter fencing, multi-layer access controls (badges, biometrics), CCTV with at least 90 days of footage retention, and formal visitor management. Only authorised personnel may enter.

title: Environmental Controls
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement environmental controls in data centres including temperature and humidity monitoring, water leak detection, and air quality management. Environmental conditions shall be continuously monitored with automated alerting when thresholds are breached.
translation: Data centres need environmental controls for temperature, humidity, water leaks, and air quality. These must be monitored continuously with automated alerts when any parameter goes outside acceptable thresholds.

title: Power & Redundancy
verbatim: Financial institutions shall ensure that data centres are equipped with redundant power supplies, uninterruptible power supply (UPS) systems, and backup generators. Power redundancy shall be designed to sustain operations during power outages with a minimum runtime of seventy-two hours for backup generators.
translation: Data centres must have redundant power including UPS systems and backup generators. Generators must be able to sustain operations for at least 72 hours during a power outage. Regular testing of power failover must be conducted.

translation: Data centres need fire detection and suppression systems suitable for protecting electronic equipment (such as gas-based suppression). Systems must be tested yearly and staff must be trained on what to do in a fire emergency.

Email authentication protocols such as SPF, DKIM, and DMARC shall be implemented for all organisational domains to prevent email spoofing.
translation: Implement comprehensive email security: spam filters, malware scanning, phishing detection, and DLP. Deploy SPF, DKIM, and DMARC on all your domains to prevent attackers from spoofing your email addresses.

title: DLP Controls
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement data loss prevention controls to detect and prevent unauthorised transmission or exfiltration of sensitive data. DLP policies shall be aligned with the institution's data classification framework and shall cover email, web, endpoint, and cloud channels.
translation: Deploy DLP tools to detect and block unauthorised transfer of sensitive data. DLP policies must align with your data classification and cover all major channels: email, web browsing, endpoints, and cloud services.

title: Vulnerability Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct vulnerability assessments of all internet-facing systems at least monthly and internal systems at least quarterly. Assessments shall use industry-recognised tools and databases. Identified vulnerabilities shall be risk-rated, prioritised, and remediated within defined timeframes.
translation: Scan internet-facing systems for vulnerabilities at least monthly and internal systems at least quarterly. Use industry-standard tools, rate the severity of what you find, and fix vulnerabilities within defined timeframes based on their risk level.

title: Penetration Testing
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct penetration testing of critical systems and applications at least annually and prior to major releases. Testing shall be performed by qualified and independent testers. Findings shall be risk-rated, reported to management, and remediated within agreed timeframes.
translation: Conduct penetration testing on critical systems at least once a year and before major releases. Use qualified, independent testers. Risk-rate the findings, report them to management, and fix them within agreed timelines.

title: Red Team Exercises
verbatim: Financial institutions should conduct red team exercises to test the effectiveness of their security controls and incident response capabilities against realistic attack scenarios. Red team exercises should simulate advanced persistent threats and should be conducted by experienced professionals with appropriate rules of engagement.
translation: Consider conducting red team exercises that simulate real-world advanced attacks to test your defences and incident response. Use experienced red team professionals and define clear rules of engagement before starting.

title: Vulnerability Remediation
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a vulnerability remediation process with defined service level targets based on risk severity. Critical vulnerabilities shall be remediated within seven days, high vulnerabilities within thirty days, and medium vulnerabilities within ninety days. Exceptions shall require documented risk acceptance.
translation: Define clear remediation timelines by severity: critical vulnerabilities within 7 days, high within 30 days, medium within 90 days. If you cannot meet these timelines, you need formal risk acceptance documentation.

title: Bug Bounty & Disclosure
verbatim: Financial institutions should consider establishing a coordinated vulnerability disclosure program or bug bounty program to leverage external security researchers in identifying vulnerabilities. Such programs should define clear scope, rules of engagement, and safe harbour provisions for participating researchers.
translation: Consider running a bug bounty or coordinated vulnerability disclosure program so external security researchers can report vulnerabilities they find. Define clear scope, rules, and safe harbour provisions to encourage responsible disclosure.

title: Threat Intelligence Program
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a cyber threat intelligence program to collect, analyse, and act upon threat intelligence relevant to the financial sector. The program shall incorporate multiple intelligence sources and shall produce actionable intelligence for security operations and risk management decisions.
translation: You need a formal threat intelligence program that gathers, analyses, and acts on cyber threat information relevant to banking. Use multiple intelligence sources and produce actionable intelligence that drives security operations and risk decisions.

title: Threat Information Sharing
verbatim: Financial institutions shall participate in threat information sharing arrangements with industry peers, government agencies, and relevant information sharing and analysis centres. Shared information shall be handled in accordance with traffic light protocol or equivalent classification to protect source confidentiality.
translation: Participate in threat information sharing with industry peers, government agencies, and ISACs. Use the Traffic Light Protocol (TLP) or equivalent to handle shared information appropriately and protect the confidentiality of sources.

title: Threat Hunting
verbatim: Financial institutions should establish proactive threat hunting capabilities to identify threats that may have evaded automated detection mechanisms. Threat hunting activities should be informed by threat intelligence and should follow structured hypotheses-driven or intelligence-driven methodologies.
translation: Go beyond automated detection by proactively hunting for threats that may have slipped through. Use structured methodologies driven by threat intelligence or specific hypotheses about potential attack vectors in your environment.

title: IoC Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish processes for managing indicators of compromise (IoCs) including collection, validation, enrichment, and integration with security monitoring tools. IoCs shall be maintained in a threat intelligence platform and regularly updated to reflect the current threat landscape.
translation: Manage IoCs through a structured process: collect them, validate them, enrich them with context, and feed them into your security tools for automated detection. Keep IoCs current and maintained in a threat intelligence platform.

title: Cyber Situational Awareness
verbatim: Financial institutions shall maintain cyber situational awareness through continuous monitoring of the external threat landscape, the institution's attack surface, and emerging threats targeting the financial sector. Situational awareness reports shall be provided to senior management and security operations on a regular basis.
translation: Stay aware of the cyber threat landscape by continuously monitoring external threats, your own attack surface, and emerging threats to the financial sector. Provide regular situational awareness reports to senior management and the security team.

title: Digital Channel Security
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement security controls for all digital channels including internet banking, mobile banking, and electronic payment systems. Security controls shall address authentication, authorisation, encryption, session management, and protection against common web application vulnerabilities.

verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement security controls for mobile banking applications including code obfuscation, root and jailbreak detection, certificate pinning, secure local storage, and protection against reverse engineering. Mobile applications shall undergo security testing prior to each release.

title: API Security
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement security controls for all application programming interfaces (APIs) including authentication, authorisation, rate limiting, input validation, and encryption. APIs shall be inventoried, documented, and subject to regular security assessments. Deprecated APIs shall be decommissioned in a timely manner.
translation: Secure all APIs with authentication, authorisation, rate limiting, input validation, and encryption. Maintain an inventory of all APIs, document them, and test their security regularly. Shut down deprecated APIs promptly.

title: Customer Authentication
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement robust customer authentication mechanisms for digital services that are commensurate with the risk profile of the transaction or service. Multi-factor authentication shall be required for high-risk transactions. Authentication mechanisms shall be regularly assessed for effectiveness.
translation: Customer authentication for digital services must match the risk level of the activity. High-risk transactions require MFA. Regularly assess whether your authentication mechanisms are still effective against current threats.

title: Transaction Verification
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement transaction verification mechanisms for high-value and high-risk digital transactions. Verification shall be performed through an independent channel from the transaction initiation channel. Customers shall be provided with transaction details for verification prior to final authorisation.
translation: High-value and high-risk digital transactions need out-of-band verification — confirming the transaction through a separate channel from the one used to initiate it. Show customers the full transaction details for confirmation before final authorisation.

title: Session Management
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement secure session management for all digital services including automatic session timeout, secure session token generation, session fixation prevention, and concurrent session controls. Session tokens shall not be transmitted in URLs and shall be invalidated upon logout.
translation: Implement secure session management for digital services: automatic timeouts, securely generated session tokens, protection against session fixation attacks, and controls on concurrent sessions. Never put session tokens in URLs and always invalidate them on logout.

title: Fraud Detection
verbatim: Financial institutions shall implement real-time fraud detection and monitoring systems for digital banking transactions. The systems shall employ behavioural analytics, transaction pattern analysis, and anomaly detection to identify potentially fraudulent activities. Suspicious transactions shall be flagged for review and potential blocking.
translation: Deploy real-time fraud detection for digital transactions using behavioural analytics, pattern analysis, and anomaly detection. Flag suspicious transactions for review and be able to block them in real time when necessary.

Digital onboarding controls should be commensurate with the risk level of the products and services being offered.

title: QR Code & E-Wallet Security

Security controls shall ensure the integrity and authenticity of QR code transactions.
translation: Secure QR code payments and e-wallet services against tampering, overlay attacks, and forgery. Ensure QR codes are validated, transactions are encrypted, and the integrity and authenticity of every QR-based transaction is verified.

title: IT Audit Plan
verbatim: Financial institutions shall establish a risk-based IT audit plan that covers all critical technology domains including cybersecurity, IT operations, change management, access control, and business continuity. The audit plan shall be approved by the audit committee and shall be executed by qualified IT auditors.
translation: Create a risk-based IT audit plan covering all key technology areas. The plan must be approved by the audit committee and executed by qualified IT auditors who understand the technology being audited.

title: IT Audit Independence
verbatim: Financial institutions shall ensure that IT audit functions maintain independence from the activities they audit. IT auditors shall have unrestricted access to all systems, records, and personnel necessary for audit activities. The internal audit function shall report directly to the audit committee.
translation: IT auditors must be independent from the teams and systems they audit. They need unrestricted access to all systems, data, and people for their work. The audit function must report directly to the audit committee, not to IT management.

Continuous professional development should be provided and tracked for all IT audit personnel.

Management shall provide action plans with target dates for all audit findings. Overdue findings shall be escalated to senior management and the audit committee.

title: Third-Party Security Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions shall obtain independent security assessments of critical third-party service providers at least annually. Assessments shall evaluate the service provider's security controls, data protection practices, and compliance with contractual security requirements. Assessment results shall be reviewed by the institution's risk management function.
translation: Get independent security assessments of your critical third-party providers at least yearly. Evaluate their security controls, data protection, and compliance with your contractual requirements. Your risk management team must review the results.

Certification status shall be monitored on an ongoing basis and lapses in certification shall trigger a reassessment of the service provider's risk profile.

The program shall include mandatory annual training on cybersecurity risks, data protection, and the institution's security policies. Training completion shall be tracked and reported.

Simulations shall be conducted at least quarterly and shall include follow-up training for personnel who fail the simulation. Metrics shall be tracked and reported to management.

title: Material Incident Notification
verbatim: Financial institutions shall notify Bank Negara Malaysia of all material technology and cybersecurity incidents within four hours of detection. The notification shall include the nature of the incident, systems affected, estimated impact, and initial response actions. A detailed follow-up report shall be submitted within fourteen days.
translation: Notify BNM within 4 hours of detecting any material technology or cybersecurity incident. Include what happened, what systems are affected, estimated impact, and what you are doing about it. Submit a detailed follow-up report within 14 days.

title: AI & Machine Learning Governance
verbatim: Financial institutions should establish governance frameworks for the use of artificial intelligence and machine learning in decision-making processes. The framework should address model risk management, data quality, bias detection, explainability, and accountability. AI models used for customer-facing decisions should be subject to regular validation.
translation: If you use AI or ML for decision-making, establish a governance framework covering model risk, data quality, bias, explainability, and accountability. AI models that affect customers should be regularly validated to ensure fairness and accuracy.

title: Emerging Technology Risk Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions should conduct risk assessments for the adoption of emerging technologies including distributed ledger technology, quantum computing preparedness, and Internet of Things deployments. Risk assessments should consider security, privacy, regulatory, and operational implications prior to adoption.
translation: Before adopting emerging technologies like blockchain, IoT, or preparing for quantum computing impacts, conduct risk assessments covering security, privacy, regulatory, and operational implications. Understand the risks before committing.
evidence: Completed risk assessments for adopted emerging technologies

title: Annual RMiT Self-Assessment
verbatim: Financial institutions shall conduct an annual self-assessment of their compliance with the Risk Management in Technology policy document. The self-assessment shall cover all applicable policy requirements and shall identify areas of non-compliance, partial compliance, and full compliance with supporting evidence.
translation: Conduct a formal annual self-assessment of your compliance with the full RMiT policy. For each requirement, determine whether you are fully compliant, partially compliant, or non-compliant, and provide supporting evidence for your assessment.
evidence: Compliance status for each RMiT clause
Board or senior management review of assessment results

artifacts.description: Supporting evidence for compliance ratings across all clauses

artifacts.description: Board or senior management review and sign-off of self-assessment results

title: Gap Remediation Planning
verbatim: Financial institutions shall develop remediation plans for all identified gaps in compliance with the Risk Management in Technology policy. Remediation plans shall include specific actions, accountable owners, target completion dates, and required resources. Progress on remediation shall be reported to the board at least quarterly.
translation: For every compliance gap found in the self-assessment, create a remediation plan with specific actions, assigned owners, target dates, and resource requirements. Report remediation progress to the board at least quarterly.
evidence: Gap remediation plan with actions and owners
Target completion dates and resource requirements
Quarterly progress reports to the board
